title: The King Is Alive
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 03-31-24 - The King is Alive - Luke 24_1-12 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. The Empty Tomb: The Evidence for the Resurrection
subpoints: The women came very early on the first day of the week, bringing spices to complete Jesus's burial — finding the stone rolled away and the body gone
The empty tomb is an undeniable historical fact not disputed by anyone of consequence; the question is only how to explain it

Four alternative theories, each less credible than the biblical account: (1) enemies hid the body — they would have produced it when disciples preached the resurrection; (2) disciples hid the body — people do not die for something they know is a lie; (3) Jesus merely swooned and recovered — a spear to the heart followed by recovery in a cold tomb is impossible; (4) a look-alike was crucified — the most recent theory, embraced by no serious historian
The simplest, most credible explanation: the Son of God rose from the dead by the power of the Father and the Spirit

outline.title: 2. The Angels: The Word as the Answer to Confusion
subpoints: The women were 'greatly perplexed' — operating under the assumption that someone had moved the body; Mary Magdalene went to tell Peter 'they have taken away the Lord and we do not know where they have laid him' (John 20:2)
God sent angels not to perform a sign but to remind the women of Jesus's own words: 'Why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here — he is risen. Remember how he spoke to you?'
In times of complexity and confusion, the simplest and best answer is the one presented by the Word of God — the Bible settles matters independently of whether we choose to believe it
The women remembered his words, and that remembrance was the beginning of understanding

outline.title: 3. The Women as First Witnesses: Luke's Emphasis
subpoints: Luke names the women: Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James — their testimony was the first witness to the resurrection
In first-century culture, women were considered unreliable witnesses and held lower social status than farm animals; their testimony would have been dismissed in court

Luke consistently elevates the role of women in his Gospel and Acts; it is the kind of detail that is only true because it happened — no one inventing the story would choose women as the first witnesses
Similarly, at Christ's birth the first witnesses were shepherds — the lowest social group in Palestine; God consistently chooses the lowly

outline.title: 4. The Disciples' Unbelief and Peter's Hope
subpoints: The apostles dismissed the women's report as 'idle tales' and did not believe them — the disciples themselves were slow to believe the resurrection
The angels specifically sent the women to 'tell Peter' — the beginning of the restoration of Peter after his three denials, which culminates in John 21
Peter arose and ran to the tomb — something remarkable, given the emotional weight he carried; hope grabbed him and he ran
Peter found the linen cloths lying alone and departed marveling — he did not yet understand, but he could not dismiss what he saw

outline.title: 5. The Resurrection and Its Implications
subpoints: Why rise bodily? Luke 24:36-46: Jesus appeared in the upper room, showed his hands and feet, ate fish — providing physical evidence that this was not a ghost; John was so struck that 50 years later he opened 1 John without a greeting: 'That which we have seen and heard and handled...'
The resurrection is the Father's declaration that the sacrifice of Jesus was accepted and sin no longer has dominion over believers
The resurrection crowns Jesus as King of kings; Ephesians 1 and 3: the same power that raised Christ from the dead is at work in every believer right now
Thomas missed the first resurrection appearance because he had forsaken the assembly; we meet the risen Christ every Lord's Day — Easter is not uniquely special

summary: A resurrection Sunday sermon on Luke 24:1-12. The women came early to complete Jesus's burial, found the stone rolled away and the body gone — a historically undeniable fact. Four alternative explanations are examined and dismissed: enemies hiding the body, disciples stealing it, Jesus merely swooning, or a look-alike being crucified. The biblical explanation is simply the most credible. When the confused women searched for answers, God sent angels not with signs but with a reminder of Jesus's own words — the Word of God is the answer in times of confusion. Luke's emphasis on women as first witnesses is noted: in a culture where women's testimony was legally worthless, only truth explains their being named as first witnesses. Peter ran to the tomb grabbing hold of hope; he marveled but did not yet fully understand. The sermon closes with implications: the risen Jesus ate fish to prove he was physical (John records the impact on the aged John in 1 John 1); the resurrection is the Father's declaration that the sacrifice was accepted; and the same resurrection power works in every believer today (Ephesians 1, 3).
transcript:
## The Third Day

This is the third day since the crucifixion — though for the disciples, huddled in the upper room in grief and fear, those three days did not feel like they passed quickly. Luke 24:17 later captures their mood: the two disciples on the road to Emmaus describe their hope in the past tense — 'we were hoping that it was he who was going to redeem Israel.' Three years of following Jesus, and now he is dead, and it has already been three days since the crucifixion.

That is where we enter the story.

## 1. The Empty Tomb

Very early on the first day of the week, the women came to the tomb. They had bought spices on Saturday night — embalming materials to complete Jesus's burial, since Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus had worked quickly before the Sabbath began. They worried as they walked about the heavy stone. When they arrived, the stone was already rolled away.

They went in and did not find the body.

The empty tomb is an undeniable historical fact — not disputed by anyone of consequence, atheist or Christian. The debate has always been: why was the tomb empty?

Four explanations have been offered over the centuries.

First: the enemies hid the body, to prevent the disciples from worshipping at the tomb. The problem: when the disciples started preaching the resurrection, the enemies had every incentive to produce the body and end the movement. They never did.

Second: the disciples stole the body — this is actually what the Jewish elders told the soldiers to say (Matthew 28:13). The problem: it is very difficult to die for something you know is a lie. Every one of the apostles gave his life for the claim that Jesus rose from the dead.

Third: Jesus never actually died — he swooned, recovered in the cool of the tomb, and walked out. This theory requires believing that a man who received a spear through his heart, was scourged until near death, and had nails through his hands and feet, recovered enough in a sealed tomb to roll away a stone from the inside and overpower the guards. It collapses under its own weight.

Fourth: a look-alike was crucified. This is the most recent theory, associated with certain Islamic traditions. It has no first-century evidence and is held by no serious historian.

The most credible explanation — simpler and more consistent with the evidence than any of the alternatives — is the one the Bible offers: the Son of God was raised from the dead by the Father in the power of the Spirit.

## 2. The Word as the Answer to Confusion

The women were 'greatly perplexed.' They did not yet think 'resurrection' — they thought 'someone moved the body.' We know this from John 20:2, where Mary Magdalene ran to Peter saying: 'They have taken away the Lord out of the tomb and we do not know where they have laid him.'

And God sent angels — not to perform a miracle, not to produce a sign, but to remind them of what Jesus had already said.

'Why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here, but he is risen. Remember how he spoke to you when he was still in Galilee, saying the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and the third day rise again.'

Duh. That is what the angels are saying, in the most loving possible way. You already know this. He told you. Remember?

In times of confusion, in times of complexity, the simplest and best answer is the one the Word of God presents. The Bible is not true because you believe it. The Bible is true because God spoke it. It settles questions independently of whether any of us choose to accept it. And when the women were reminded of his words, they remembered — and went back.

## 3. The Women as First Witnesses

Luke names them: Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the others with them.

This is a remarkable fact. In first-century Jewish culture, a woman's testimony was not admissible in court. Women were considered unreliable witnesses — their social status was lower than that of farm animals in some respects. No one inventing the resurrection story would have chosen women as the first witnesses. The choice undermines the credibility of the claim in its own cultural context.

The women are first witnesses precisely because it is true. That is the only explanation.

Luke makes this point repeatedly in his Gospel and in Acts — the two largest books of the New Testament. Women are consistently elevated in Luke's writings: as witnesses, as disciples, as bearers of faith. The gospel frees women to serve and to witness in ways that their culture refused to recognize.

## 4. Peter's Hope

The apostles heard the women's report and dismissed it as idle tales. They did not believe.

But something interesting happened in Peter. The angels had specifically told the women: go, tell the disciples — and tell Peter. Peter, who had denied Jesus three times. Peter, who was carrying the full weight of that shame. The message came to him first: your Lord is alive. The restoration of Peter — which culminates in John 21, with the threefold commission to feed the Lord's sheep — begins at the empty tomb.

Peter arose and ran.

He ran. That is not the posture of someone who is indifferent. That is the posture of someone who has grabbed hold of hope and will not let go. John ran with him. John got there first. Peter ran straight in.

And he found the linen cloths lying alone. He departed marveling.

He did not yet understand. But he could not look away.

## 5. The Risen Christ

Later that same day, the risen Christ appeared to the disciples in the upper room. Luke 24:36-46. He said: Peace be with you. They thought he was a ghost. He said: look at my hands and feet. Touch me. A spirit does not have flesh and bones. And he asked for a snack — a piece of broiled fish and honeycomb.

Why did he ask for food? Because of love. He gave them physical, tangible, undeniable evidence that he had risen bodily. This experience struck John so deeply that fifty years later, when he wrote what we call 1 John, he didn't open with 'Dear friends.' He opened with: 'That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we have looked upon, and our hands have handled, concerning the Word of life.' I touched him. I heard him. I saw him.

The resurrection is the Father's declaration that the sacrifice of the Son was accepted — that sin no longer has dominion over those who believe in the risen Christ. It is the crowning of Jesus as King of kings and Lord of lords, with all authority in heaven and on earth.

And Ephesians 1 and 3 tell us: the same power that raised Christ from the dead is working in you right now. Present tense. The resurrection is not only a historical event you look back at. It is the power you live by today.

Your Savior lives.

Father, thank you for your Word, and for the living Savior it announces. Do not let us leave here and forget that our Lord is risen and reigns over us. In his name, amen.
